issue_title: Tiny race condition in curio.local
issue_body:
Theoretically, someone might be calling `curio.run` simultaneously from two different threads, and theoretically, those two runs might both end up using some code from a single library, and theoretically, that library might contain a `curio.Local`. And if all that were true, then theoretically it might happen that the two threads both attempted to access some attribute of that `curio.Local` object at the same time.

If this were to happen, then it's possible that one thread would see the other thread's local values, because currently `curio.Local` mutates the object in-place (see `_patch_magic_dict`) and doesn't have any locking.

I doubt anyone has hit this in real life, but since I noticed it I figured I'd mention it :-)

issue_body:
That is a nasty issue, and it even contradicts the name of the project...
As [Alex](https://github.com/alex-robinson) mentioned to me the other day, @szaghi did a lot of work in FoBiS.py to work-out concepts, a workflow, a user-interface...and actually very little of this is really Fortran specific. Why not extend this to, say, C/C++ ? 
As a disclaimer: I am not a C/C++ programmer, so I cannot even help. I was just curious, especially when searching for libraries the other day, that only existed in C++. - workout compiler options ... ( -compiler gnu => g++)

My 2 cents (talking...)

issue_title: Wrong links get displayed when typing "howdoi cat --all -n 3"
issue_body:
```
>howdoi cat --all -n 3
★  Answer from http://rads.stackoverflow.com/amzn/click/B000RIA95G ★
While cat does stand for "concatenate", what it actually does is simply display one or multiple files, in order of their appearance in the command line arguments to cat . The common pattern to view the contents of a file on Linux or *nix systems is:
cat <file>
The main difference between cat and Git's cat-file is that it only displays a single file (hence the -file part). Git's cat-file doesn't really stand for "concatenate"; it simply is a reference to the behavior of the cat command.
git-cat-file - Provide content or type and size information for repository objects
Technically, you can use git cat-file to concatenate files, if you use Batch Output mode:
BATCH OUTPUT If --batch or --batch-check is given, cat-file will read objects from stdin, one per line, and print information about them. By default, the whole line is considered as an object, as if it were fed to git-rev-parse [1].

★  Answer from http://rads.stackoverflow.com/amzn/click/0679449531 ★
the problem is that you are using a Fileconnection in combination with cat then the append won't work. There are several option you could use, the most easy one is to this: first "create" the file, if you want to add a header for example:
header = "some header" 
## if you don't want to use a header then leave the header blank
header =""
cat(text, file="outputR.txt", append=FALSE, sep = "\n")
notice the append = FALSE this is necessary if you want to clear the file if it already exist otherwise you have to use append = TRUE the you can write text to it using:
text = text = paste(toString(id), ":", toString(goIDs))
cat(text file="outputR.txt", append=TRUE, sep = "\n")

★  Answer from http://rads.stackoverflow.com/amzn/click/B003E6WSHK ★
$ cat > hello.c
#include <stdio.h>

main(void) {
    printf("Hello, world.\n");
}
^D
$ gcc hello.c
$ ./a.out
Hello, world.
```

If I request the actual links though...
```
>howdoi cat -l -n 3
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/38177026/what-does-cat-file-stand-for-in-git

https://stackoverflow.com/questions/39942297/r-doesnt-append-lines-to-file-using-cat-or-write

https://stackoverflow.com/questions/921455/how-do-i-program-using-cat
```

issue_title: Order of records parsed by gffutils?
issue_body:
Hi Ryan and all,

Finally back to hacking on gffutils (over at my fork; will send pull request when next batch of features are completed.) I notice that in the `refactor` branch I forked today, the unit test `test_attributes_modify`. I believe it is because the GFF records are not read in the order in which they appear in the GFF file, like they used to be. The test function is below. The output I get when running it is:

```
$ python gffutils/test/test.py
First child is not an mRNA
exon
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "gffutils/test/test.py", line 1073, in <module>
    test_attributes_modify()
  File "gffutils/test/test.py", line 1061, in test_attributes_modify
    assert str(gene_childs[0].attributes) == 'ID=FBtr0300689;Name=CG11023-RB;Parent=FBgn0031208;Dbxref=FlyBase_Annotation_IDs:CG11023-RB;score_text=Strongly Supported;score=11'
AssertionError
```

If I understand it correctly, I believe it's reading the exon record before the mRNA record, although in the file the mRNA appears as the first child of the gene:

```
chr2L	FlyBase	gene	7529	9484	.	+	.	ID=FBgn0031208;Name=CG11023;Ontology_term=SO:0000010,SO:0000087,GO:0008234,GO:0006508;Dbxref=FlyBase:FBan0011023,FlyBase_Annotation_IDs:CG11023,GB:AE003590,GB_protein:AAO41164,GB:AI944728,GB:AJ564667,GB_protein:CAD92822,GB:BF495604,UniProt/TrEMBL:Q6KEV3,UniProt/TrEMBL:Q86BM6,INTERPRO:IPR003653,BIOGRID:59420,dedb:5870,GenomeRNAi_gene:33155,ihop:59383;derived_computed_cyto=21A5-21A5;gbunit=AE014134
chr2L	FlyBase	mRNA	7529	9484	.	+	.	ID=FBtr0300689;Name=CG11023-RB;Parent=FBgn0031208;Dbxref=FlyBase_Annotation_IDs:CG11023-RB;score_text=Strongly Supported;score=11
```

Although the GFF format is unordered, I think it's worth ensuring that the entries are read in the order in which they appear in the file. We at least use this convention heavily to always put the longest mRNA entries first and these types of conventions are very useful.

I believe that the unordered entries are returned by the `children` method and that in general gffutils stores entries in the file appearance order. Here's a self-contained example (note that `get_db_fname` is a function I'm contributing to `helpers` in my fork):

```
In [56]: f = gffutils.example_filename("FBgn0031208.gff")

In [57]: db_fname = helpers.get_db_fname(f)

In [58]: db = gffutils.FeatureDB(db_fname)

In [59]: all_recs = list(db.all())

# Gene and mRNA are read first (file appearance order)
In [61]: all_recs[0:2]
Out[61]: [<Feature: gene, chr2L:7529-9484 (+)>, <Feature: mRNA, chr2L:7529-9484 (+)>]

# But the first child is not an mRNA, it is an exon
In [62]: c = list(db.children("FBgn0031208"))

In [63]: c[0]
Out[63]: <Feature: exon, chr2L:7529-8116 (+)>
```

The test function is:

```
def test_attributes_modify():
    """
    Test that attributes can be modified in a GFF record.

    TODO: This test case fails?
    """
    # Test that attributes can be modified
    gffutils.create_db(gffutils.example_filename('FBgn0031208.gff'), testdbfn_gff,
                       verbose=False,
                       force=True)
    db = gffutils.FeatureDB(testdbfn_gff)
    gene_id = "FBgn0031208"
    gene_childs = list(db.children(gene_id))
    print "First child is not an mRNA"
    print gene_childs[0].featuretype
    assert str(gene_childs[0].attributes) == 'ID=FBtr0300689;Name=CG11023-RB;Parent=FBgn0031208;Dbxref=FlyBase_Annotation_IDs:CG11023-RB;score_text=Strongly Supported;score=11'
    gene_childs[0].attributes["ID"] = "Modified"
    assert str(gene_childs[0].attributes) == 'ID=Modified;Name=CG11023-RB;Parent=FBgn0031208;Dbxref=FlyBase_Annotation_IDs:CG11023-RB;score_text=Strongly Supported;score=11;ID=Modified'
```

issue_title: Add @Py.group annotation
Error in gensim prepare when corpus is passed as a matrix
issue_body:
A test is present in the `_extract_data` function in `gensim.py`, presumably so that the corpus can be passed as a sparse matrix:

``` .python
if not gensim.matutils.ismatrix(corpus):
      corpus_csc = gensim.matutils.corpus2csc(corpus)
   else:
      corpus_csc = corpus
```

Later, however, the length of the corpus is tested:

``` .python
   assert doc_lengths.shape[0] == len(corpus), 'Document lengths and corpus have different sizes {} != {}'.format(doc_lengths.shape[0], len(corpus))
```

When corpus is a sparse matrix, `len(corpus)` will raise an exception because the length of a sparse matrix is ambiguous.

issue_title: Recent commit in Tesseract 4 break Tesserocr installation
issue_body:
**This only applies to Tesseract 4**

This is an easy problem to solve but people might hit a wall if they aren't aware.

Commit da03e4e [see here](https://github.com/tesseract-ocr/tesseract/commit/da03e4e9105b6262706d40ef2b4436eae4ebe19f#diff-4376fff11de6717e265623d40f1b0820) added the use of std::string in the unichar.h header file that is used during compilation by `tesserocr`. Unfortunately (and I'm not sure why, explanations are welcome) the standard library (std) is **not** included. This caused the compiler to throw an error

    In file included from tesserocr.cpp:484:0:
    /usr/local/include/tesseract/unichar.h:164:10: error: ‘string’ does not name a type
    static string UTF32ToUTF8(const std::vector<char32>& str32);
              ^

**Changing**  `static string UTF32ToUTF8(const std::vector<char32>& str32);` **to** `static string UTF32ToUTF8(const std::vector<char32>& str32);` **in** `/usr/local/include/tesseract/unichar.h:164` **fixes the problem for me.**

According to a comment that can be seen [here if you scroll a bit](https://github.com/tesseract-ocr/tesseract/commit/aee910a7bfcbe0366806b68dc7f20535764c06d4) Google seems to have a problem with **std::string** vs **string** so while I will open an issue on their repo, I feel it is unlikely that someone will fix it. Therefore I recommend using my "dirty hack".

Anyone with more knowledge than me is very welcome to clarify / propose something else.

issue_title: Inaccurate reporting of process creation time via create_time() under Linux
issue_body:
It looks like the value reported in psutil via `create_time()` is inaccurate under Linux. It's too far in the past (by less than a second, in my tests). It looks like the offset is constant across various runs. I only tested this on Linux (both debian and arch). The issue appears in both python2 and python3.

I wrote a sample script to illustrate the problem. The script forks a new process and then fetches the new process' creation time. You'll notice that the reported `create_time()` of the forked process is too far back in the past: older than the current timestamp at the beginning of the script. For comparison, I also printed out the timestamp for the folder creation obtained via the `stat` command on `/proc/<pid>` (well, modification time, I couldn't get the creation time for some reason, but this is not relevant to the issue).

``` python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import subprocess
import datetime

import psutil

PROCESS_TO_FORK = "python3"


def main():
    initial_time = datetime.datetime.now()
    print("time now: {}".format(initial_time))
    p = subprocess.Popen(
        PROCESS_TO_FORK,
        stdout=subprocess.DEVNULL,
        stderr=subprocess.DEVNULL)
    proc = psutil.Process(p.pid)
    stat_out = subprocess.check_output(
        ['stat', '-c', '%y', '/proc/{}'.format(p.pid)])

    ct = datetime.datetime.fromtimestamp(proc.create_time())
    print("create_time from psutil: {}".format(ct))
    print("create_time from stat: {}".format(stat_out.decode("utf-8")))
    print("process uptime according to psutil: {}".format(
        (datetime.datetime.now() - ct).total_seconds()))

    end_time = datetime.datetime.now()
    print("time now: {}".format(end_time))
    print("script runtime: {}s".format(
        (end_time - initial_time).total_seconds()))


if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()
```

A sample output would be:

```
$ time ./test_create.py
time now: 2016-08-29 14:38:52.471206
create_time from psutil: 2016-08-29 14:38:51.930000
create_time from stat: 2016-08-29 14:38:52.469848115 +0200

process uptime according to psutil: 0.543344
time now: 2016-08-29 14:38:52.473368
script runtime: 0.002162s
./test_create.py  0.07s user 0.00s system 97% cpu 0.072 total
```

As can be seen from the output, the process runtime is larger than the script runtime, which is impossible. Note that the time obtained from `stat` is also a bit in the past compared to the initial timestamp, so that's also wrong, but it's not off by as much. The fact that the resolution of create_time() seems to be many digits is misleading if its accuracy is actually within 1s.

Other tools seem to only show process creation-time/runtime up to a second accuracy. Is it impossible to get a more fine grained value? It seems unlikely because the offset/error is [almost] constant.

Reference: http://unix.stackexchange.com/questions/7870/how-to-check-how-long-a-process-has-been-running

issue_title: Assignment attempted on something that is not index based
Detect url scheme when behind proxy
issue_body:
I'm running Django behind a nginx-ingress as a proxy which also handles HTTPS termination. This leads to the problem that drf-yasg autodetecs HTTP scheme as the connection between Django and nginx is HTTP. Therefore the generated swagger file points to the HTTP scheme and the swagger-ui is not correctly working as the browser blocks mixed content requests.

```
Mixed Content: The page at 'https://www.example.com/api/swagger/' was loaded over HTTPS, but requested an insecure resource 'http:///www.example.com/api/user/'. This request has been blocked; the content must be served over HTTPS.
```

I don't want to set `DEFAULT_API_URL` as Django runs inside a container and this container does not and should not know the hostnames it serves or the upstream protocol behind proxy.

This could be resolved by using the [`X-Forwarded-Proto`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers/X-Forwarded-Proto) HTTP header, which is set by the upstream proxy, in the `OpenAPISchemaGenerator` class.

issue_title: Support for encoding/decoding non-string messages
issue_body:
I've been trying to figure out how to send Avro-encoded messages with kafka-python. As best as I can tell, this isn't possible, since the `write_int_string ` method called by `_encode_message` checks to make sure that the message is of type String, and errors out if this is not the case.

I don't see any reason why the kafka-python Producer should require that the message be a string, since Kafka messages are just bytes. Why was this design decision made, and is this something that's easily changeable to support non-string messages?

Or, am I just missing something, and this is possible with the current release of kafka-python?

issue_title: New client imports are extremely confusing
issue_body:
I've been playing around with current master and I recently just got bitten hard by this recent change to the module init:

```
from kafka.client import KafkaClient as SimpleClient
from kafka.client_async import KafkaClient
```

I realize that this will probably be a little less difficult to get hurt by when the full release is out and the changelog is updated (and hopefully major version bumped), but this is very counterintuitive. I have `from kafka import KafkaClient` in my application code, and in any normal situation I'd be able to grep for `class KafkaClient` in the library and find the class I want.

I'm wondering if this change is necessary and if it's worth the obfuscation. If there is going to be a backwards-incompatible change either way, would it make more sense to import them as `SimpleClient` and `AsyncClient`?

issue_title: mean(radius) for correlation function bins
issue_body: For 2pt correlations, the separation value currently returned is meanlogr, i.e. the mean of the log separation of the pairs in the bin. It would be useful to have the actual (weighted) mean separation, since I believe this is the separation at which you want to compute the theoretical signal you're comparing to. For wide bins the difference can be significant.

issue_title: Improved quality of NER with Swedish
issue_body:
I just found polyglot, which seems to be FANTASTIC for dealing with all sorts of NLP problems in multiple languages. I want to use it for Swedish texts so I got to work and tested it on some real world texts.

Here's a random swedish article: http://www.dn.se/nyheter/sverige/oligarken-som-ager-en-o-i-stockholm/ I manually copy-pasted the text into a txt file, downloaded the required swedish models and tried to get the NER tags from it. 

```python
from polyglot.text import Text
text = Text(open("test.txt").read())
for entity in text.entities:
    print(entity.tag, entity)
```

Here's a part of the ouput:
...
I-LOC ['Stockholm']
I-LOC ['slovakisk']
I-PER ['Frantisek']
I-PER ['Jules', 'Verne']
I-PER ['Zvrskovec']
I-LOC ['Lidingö']
I-PER ['Bilspedition']
I-LOC ['Tjeckien']
I-PER ['oligarken']
I-LOC ['Indiana']
I-PER ['Indiana', 'Jones']
I-ORG ['Arlanda']
I-LOC ['Tjeckien']
I-LOC ['Stockholm']
I-PER ['Frantisek', 'Zvrskovec']
I-PER ['.']
I-PER ['Frantisek', 'Zvrskovec']
I-PER ['bottenskrevan']
I-LOC ['Stockholms']
I-PER ['landstigningsförbud']
I-PER ['helstängt']
I-PER ['Magnus', 'Hallgren']
I-PER ['Dividend']
I-ORG ['Central', 'Europe']
I-PER ['Zvrskovec']
I-PER ['.']
I-LOC ['Tjeckoslovakien']
I-LOC ['Dolny']
I-PER ['Dolny', 'Kubin']
I-PER ['.']
...

...which is ok, but two things stand out:

1. Lots of People tags are just punctuation. Is this a known bug in polyglot?
2. All the lowercase words in the example above are actually just nouns, and not people (let me know if you need a translation of the words to make sense of them). Is that also a bug in polyglot?

I guess I could write my own filter to remove punctuation and lowercase words, but this seems should be easier to solve in an earlier step, when training the models, don't you think?

### Environment
- Python: 2.7.6
- pyodbc: 4.0.23

- DB: SQL Server 2012
- driver: FreeTDS
Driver=FreeTDS;Server=mytest.com;port=1433;uid=MyUser;pwd=MyPwd!;database=TEST;TDS_Version=8.0

query = "UPDATE testObject SET ApartmentNumber='11' OUTPUT INSERTED.ID WHERE ID='FC570587-9CE9-4003-B0DA-EE3A1DD7BF0C'"

issue_title: Drop ng-cordova
issue_body:
The ng-cordova wrapper library has yet to fully support Cordova 5.2 and related plugins, such as `cordova-plugin-appversion`.

Since not many of Cordova's "core" plugins are used, ng-cordova and the corresponding ng-cordova-mocks may be dropped altogether, since platform-specifics should be handled with Cordova "merges" anyway.

issue_title: mpd_status creates a new socket connection every second
issue_body:
I'm not sure if this should be a bug report or a feature request, but I've been bitten by this, erm, _feature_: I use `mopidy` (mpd with spotify integration) and that gets confused when a request comes in from `mpd_status` while it processes a mpd-command-block. 

This also keeps a lot of abandoned connections in `TIME_WAIT` state - 60 currently at my system. 

I've had a quick look at the code for this module, and the connection i s esatablished in `current_track()`. I do not know if it is possible, but the best apporach (IMHO) would be to `connect()` once and keep the socket alive until `py3status` gets reloaded.

issue_title: feature selection optimization
issue_body:
most important is to see if there's a more accurate way to do feature selection. 

secondly, we can probably speed up training time a bit by training a feature selection model once per pipeline, and feeding that in, rather than having to fit a new one for each new call to .fit(). 

runs into the obvious issue of not having the data in the right format at the start (dictionaries, vs. sparse matrices). 

maybe, again, we create a natural jumping off point for auto_ml, where it can exit the process at this step, and we can feed that into  our new outer auto_ml. 

so, we could potentially train up an auto_ml that does nothing more than the basic data transforms, and feature selection. this half-auto_ml then gets inserted into a parent pipeline as the first half of that parent pipeline. this saves us from having to do any of the fitting of that half pipeline again- we can just return self from .fit, and have .transform already prepared.

issue_title: Add compatibility with Python 2
issue_body:
Cf. https://twitter.com/aymericaugustin/status/713008267165827072

The README explains what changes are required for running the code on Python 2.

I want to enforce keyword-only arguments in the API and they aren't supported on Python 2. (`kwonly` is overkill here.)

I believe the most elegant solution would be to make these changes on the fly during installation, only on Python 2. If someone wants to extend setup.py to do that, I'll consider a pull request favorably.

Since I'm not planning to use Python 2 again, I don't have any interest in writing that code myself.

issue_title: Use VS2008 to build the subprocess executable to avoid false positives by AVs
issue_body:
v56.1 uses VS2015 to build the subprocess executable and there are a few false positives reported on virustotal.com:
https://virustotal.com/en/file/4a035e71be247a67ca0ab8267f5900aeff8c740a660a92e5cd183ec6e710027e/analysis/

v31.2 was built with VS2008 and virustotal detectedion ratio is 0/61:
https://virustotal.com/en/file/3487888da5cc8790c327b2fed3d4432319c059428dd984737df1448b65bf7305/analysis/1490807371/

Using VS2008 should fix the problem, however since VS2015 is much newer compiler it might have better performance. The /LARGEADDRESSAWARE flag used when building subprocess is supported also in VS 2008.

Initial report on the Forum:
https://groups.google.com/d/topic/cefpython/0jseNIjCqW4/discussion

issue_title: Context not being reset between requests
issue_body:
My tests typically require making a number of requests to my app, and what I'm seeing is that within my app, variables attached to Flask's g are retained across requests.

I believe the problem is due to the fact that the `self._ctx.push()` is called in `self._pre_setup()` prior to the test case being run and then `self._ctx.pop()` is called after the test case runs in `self._post_teardown()`. This appears to keep the context around for the duration of the test case, rather than allowing it to be scoped to the request.

Commenting out these 2 calls fixes the problem for me. 

I'm not sure if this is related to #38 since I'm not doing anything with templates, but context variables sticking to the first request sounds like the same thing.

issue_title: Ellipsis in arg raises ValueError
issue_body:
I'm on mockito 1.1.0

```
File "mockito/signature.py" line 45 in match_signature
    if Ellipsis in args:
ValueError: The truth value of an array with more than one element is ambiguous. Use a.any() or a.all()
```

Here's some code that reproduces the bug:

```
import numpy as np
import unittest

from mockito import when, arg_that


class MakeMeFail:
    def run(self, array: np.ndarray) -> int:
        return 0


class TestMakeMeFail(unittest.TestCase):
    def setUp(self) -> None:
        self.__a_np_array = np.array([1, 2, 3])
        self.__make_me_fail = MakeMeFail()

    def test_this_one_fails(self) -> None:
        expected_value = -1029
        when(self.__make_me_fail)\
            .run(arg_that(lambda arg: np.array_equal(arg, self.__a_np_array)))\
            .thenReturn(expected_value)

        result = self.__make_me_fail.run(self.__a_np_array)

        self.assertEqual(expected_value, result)

    def test_it_is_caused_by_this(self) -> None:
        with self.assertRaises(ValueError):
            result = None in (self.__a_np_array, 1, 2)

    def test_this_one_works_because_kwargs_are_not_getting_the_Ellipsis_in_kwargs_check(self) -> None:
        expected_value = -1029
        when(self.__make_me_fail)\
            .run(array=arg_that(lambda arg: np.array_equal(arg, self.__a_np_array)))\
            .thenReturn(expected_value)

        result = self.__make_me_fail.run(array=self.__a_np_array)

        self.assertEqual(expected_value, result)
```


Love the module btw

issue_title: Validation errors nested in lists are broken and raise AttributeError
issue_body:
Between 0.8.9 and 0.8.10, it is no longer possible to correctly validate the content of lists. It seems that before the latest commits the inner exceptions were wrapped into a specific exception type, but now they may be propagated as MultipleInvalid exceptions. This exception type will not allow setting its `path` property, so it may cause the following error:

```python
>>> from voluptuous import *
>>> s = Schema(All([Any('one', 'two', 'three')]), Length(min=1))
>>> s(['one'])
['one']
>>> s(['four'])
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "venv/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/voluptuous.py", line 351, in __call__
    return self._compiled([], data)
  File "venv/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/voluptuous.py", line 793, in validate_callable
    error.path = path + error.path
AttributeError: can't set attribute
```

If I run from 32aeeec65d77940655a2905c9f15114586eb785e I get the correct:

```python
>>> s(['four'])
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "venv/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/voluptuous.py", line 355, in __call__
    return self._compiled([], data)
  File "venv/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/voluptuous.py", line 794, in validate_callable
    return schema(data)
  File "venv/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/voluptuous.py", line 1394, in __call__
    raise e if self.msg is None else AllInvalid(self.msg)
voluptuous.MultipleInvalid: invalid list value @ data[0]
```

I'd like to be able to catch `ExpiredSignatureError`, `DecodeError` and other specific exceptions and do something meaningful with them, but they aren't exposed for import. I can only import `JWTError`, but I see more specific exceptions 'escaping' (eg: `DecodeError`).

Further on an expired signature for example, internal to flask-jwt a `ExpiredSignatureError` is generated, but is later exposed externally as a `JWTError` with only a description that the token has expired which makes exception handling awkward as I have to inspect the text of the description rather than just catching on the exception type itself.

Can these errors be exposed externally so they can be imported and used externally?

Thanks!

issue_title: bit_width of 0 causes a crash
issue_body:
in read_data_page() of parquet/__init__.py
when the bit_width is zero, we need to implement what this class provides:
https://github.com/apache/parquet-mr/blob/e54ca615f213f5db6d34d9163c97eec98920d7a7/parquet-column/src/main/java/org/apache/parquet/column/values/rle/ZeroIntegerValuesReader.java

I have a fix for this, but I need to test this some more:
        if bit_width == 0:
            vals += [dictionary[0] for i in range(0,daph.num_values)]
        else:
            # TODO jcrobak -- not sure that this loop is needed?
            while total_seen < daph.num_values:
                values = encoding.read_rle_bit_packed_hybrid(
                    dict_values_io_obj, bit_width, len(dict_values_bytes))
                if len(values) + total_seen > daph.num_values:
                    values = values[0: daph.num_values - total_seen]
                vals += [dictionary[v] for v in values]
                total_seen += len(values)

the problem happens on a very small parquet file with, in my case, just 4 rows.
The problem row represents a user_id and all row user_ids are the same. The user id bytes are stored in the dictionary section, and from what I could gather from the java code, one is supposed to replicate the one dictionary value by `daph.num_values` times.

issue_title: ia upload from stdin accepts data faster than it uploads
issue_body:
Consider a script like the following:
```
tar -cv 100MegabyteFolder | pv -ps "100M" | ia upload identifier - --remote-name="100MegabyteFolder.tar"
```
The progress bar shown by `pv` will be wrong (it will indicate completion prematurely) when uploading using a slow Internet connection, because ia upload accepts the input from stdin faster than it uploads.

issue_title: Support for properties dict while retaining comments?
issue_body:
Hey there,

I've been looking for a full-featured library to handle Java properties and your implementation looks to be the most thorough on the internet, so +1 for that. :) My use case is that I want to be able to load a `.properties` file, change just a handful of lines, and then serialize it back to a file (while retaining comments, blank lines, etc.). I've discovered the low-level `parse` method which returns a generator of tuples, but is there an in-built way to create an object from a `.properties` file which has k-v lookup/modification along with the ability to serialize back to a file with comments? 

If not, would you be open to a PR to add such support? I'd be happy to do it if you could give me guidance on how you'd prefer the design to look.

issue_title: Proposal: Drop "from hell" from the name
issue_body:
This library is pretty great and it seems like "mwparser" would be an appropriate name.  It would make sense next to other libraries that implement the "mw<what it does>" naming scheme.  E.g. [mwapi](https://github.com/mediawiki-utilities/python-mwapi).

Advantages:

1. It's a better name.  
2. Shorter name is easier to type and would reduce the need for aliasing on import (e.g. `import mwparserfromhell as mwp`) -- a common practice.

Disadvantages:

1. Loss of backwards compatibility with scripts referencing the old name
2. Lots of docs and references would need to be updated. 

So, there's some benefits and drawbacks assuming you agree with me that that name "mwparser" is better.

issue_title: Preserve front matter ordering
issue_body:
It's a tricky thing to do with PyYAML, but using http://stackoverflow.com/a/21048064/5483904 it's possible. Once you fix the loader up, the things that stand in the way are:

* `parse` takes in defaults as `**kwargs`, which is automatically a dict. Additionally, you can't specify a custom loader, so you have to set it globally.
* Once you fix that, `Post.__init__` takes the metadata as `**kwargs` yet again, which destroys the ordered dict received by the prior function

I suspect to really do this, one would have to break backwards compat. Here's my hacky way of accomplishing this:

```
#  Patch yaml first

import collections
import yaml

_mapping_tag = yaml.resolver.BaseResolver.DEFAULT_MAPPING_TAG

def dict_representer(dumper, data):
    return dumper.represent_dict(data.items())

def dict_constructor(loader, node):
    loader.flatten_mapping(node)
    return collections.OrderedDict(loader.construct_pairs(node))

try:
    yaml.CSafeDumper.add_representer(collections.OrderedDict, dict_representer)
except ImportError:
    pass

yaml.SafeDumper.add_representer(collections.OrderedDict, dict_representer)
yaml.SafeLoader.add_constructor(_mapping_tag, dict_constructor)

# patch frontmatter second
import frontmatter

def _parse(text, **defaults):
    text = frontmatter.u(text)
    
    # metadata starts with defaults
    metadata = defaults.copy()
    
    try:
        _, fm, content = frontmatter.FM_BOUNDARY.split(text, 2)
    except ValueError:
        return metadata, text
    
    fm = yaml.safe_load(fm)
    
    if isinstance(fm, dict):
        fm.update(metadata)
        metadata = fm
        
    return metadata, content.strip()

def _loads(text, **defaults):
    metadata, content = _parse(text, **defaults)
    post = frontmatter.Post(content)
    post.metadata = metadata
    return post

frontmatter.parse = _parse
frontmatter.loads = _loads

```

issue_title: Provide simpler access from Namespace instance to an application `.app`
issue_body:
Namespace instance allows handling events using Socket.IO. When handling these
events, one very often needs custom devices, which are usually bound to the
underlying application, e.g. connections or pools of connections for a database, REDIS, proxy class instances etc.

Similar need was discussed in issue #139, that time in relation to `request` instance (which is alternative mean for accessing an Application instance).

The issue is, how to handle this rather typical requirement in clean way. Options I am thinking about are:

## Subclassed `Namespace` with `.app` property

It may look like this:

	class MyNamespace(socketio.AsyncNamespace):
	    """Namespace to handle SocketIO communication
	    """
	    def __init__(self, namespace, app):
		super().__init__(namespace=namespace)
		self.app = app

To instantiate such namespace, one has to pass `app` to it, e.g.:


	def setup(app):
	    sio = socketio.AsyncServer(async_mode='aiohttp')
	    namespace = MyNamespace("/my", app)
	    sio.register_namespace(namespace)
	    sio.attach(app)
	    app["my_ns"] = namespace


## let `sio.attach(app)` fill `.app` into namespaces

As one can see, the code above repeatedly handles the `app` value and it feels a bit too much.

The `sio.attach(app)` adds new SocketIO related route to the `app`, one can
think of adding extra step where it would iterate over all currently registered
namespaces and adding `.app` property to it.

Of course, this assumes, one namespace instance is always bound to max one
SocketIO server and this one to max one application. If this prerequisite is
void, then the solution would always have to access `app` value via `sid` of
the event being handled.

## Add NamespaceAppMixin or NamespaceRequestMixin

Using NamespaceRequestMixin for MyNamespace would either add extra parameter `app`
for the `__init__`, or would ensure, the `on_connect` and `on_disconnect`
maintains `.requests` dictionary and new methods `get_request(sid)` and
`get_app(sid)` woudl be added.

The second option may look similarly to (incomplete):

	class NamespaceRequestMixin(object):

	    def on_connect(self, sid, environ):
		self.requests[sid] = environ["aiohttp.request"]

	    def on_disconnect(self, sid):
		del self.requests[sid]

	    def get_request(self, sid):
	  	return self.requests[sid]

	    def get_app(self, sid):
		return self.get_request(sid).app

## Conclusions
All proposed solutions seems to be a bit hacky. At the moment I am most in favour of the `NamespaceRequestMixin`.

issue_title: hashivault_write always tries to read before writing, even when update=false
issue_body:
Hey,

As the title says. I can see this was changed recently but it actually breaks a number of things.

One example is the aws secret backend, which is configured at the path `aws/config/root`. But this path is write-only, so trying to read first throws an error.

Likewise for approles, you can generate a secret-id by writing to `auth/approle/role/my-role/secret-id`, but attempting to read from this path is an error.

It also fails if you simply don't have permission to read the endpoint you're trying to write to (entirely possible for automated scripts)

Can you revert the change please? Or find a way of doing the same that doesn't error in these cases?

issue_title: Possible typo in objects.py in Field.clone()
"datetime" field type translated as TIMESTAMP SQL Type
issue_body:
Why is this no longer DATETIME?

This shows quite odd behavior in MySQL: the first datetime field receives a default value (current date/time), and an update constraint (again, current datetime), and it is impossible to set it to None (because that just sets it back to the current date).

That breaks a lot of our functionality that operates with datetime fields (all of our tables do) - and it is quite unexpected a change.

Shouldn't the "TIMESTAMP" type be a separate field type if it is desired, and "datetime" be left as it was before?

issue_title: ACCURACY IS USING NUMPY, URGENT FIX
issue_body:
Accuracy is the bottleneck for even a 4000 node neural network. This is a practical job that many people will run and which a GPU ***CAN accelerate***.

But right now the accuracy metric is the bottleneck. That's because it converts everything to numpy, causing a blocking operation at every iteration. Let's fix it stat.

  
```
for label, pred_label in zip(labels, preds):
            if pred_label.shape != label.shape:
                pred_label = ndarray.argmax(pred_label, axis=self.axis)
            pred_label = pred_label.asnumpy().astype('int32')
            label = label.asnumpy().astype('int32')

            check_label_shapes(label, pred_label)

            self.sum_metric += (pred_label.flat == label.flat).sum()
            self.num_inst += len(pred_label.flat)

issue_title: Proposal: PR Template
issue_body:
# What
I’d like to propose that we use PR template in MXNet. The template should consist of a checklist for the required items for merging a PR (e.g. lint and testing), as well as the items of the change (e.g. features)

# Why
It should serve two purposes:
- It reminds both the contributor and reviewer/committers of checking the prerequisites before merging.
- It serves as a progress tracking tool on which PRs are ready for review and merging, so that reviewer/committers 

# How
- Contributor should maintain the status of PR by checking/unchecking items in the checklist.
- Committer should uncheck items that hasn’t been passed yet (e.g. contributor checked lint but lint failed on CI), and make comments on what more should be done. By default committers review PRs that are shown to be complete in the PR list.

# FAQ
- What if I as a contributor want to request for review of things working in progress?
Everyone can still do what everyone is doing now: requesting reviewer to take a look by @ them.

# Template
## Description ##
(Brief description on what this PR is about)

## Checklist ##
### Essential ###
- [ ] Passed code style checking (`make lint`)
- [ ] Changes are complete (i.e. I finished coding on this PR)
- [ ] All changes have test coverage
- [ ] Examples are either not affected by this change, or have been fixed to be compatible with this change
- [ ] For user-facing API changes, API doc string has been updated.

### Changes ###
- [ ] Feature1, tests, (and when applicable, API doc)
- [ ] Feature2, tests, (and when applicable, API doc)

## Comments ##
- If this change is a backward incompatible change, why must this change be made.
- Intersting edge cases to note here

issue_title: [WIP-NewFeature] ONNX support for MXNet
issue_body:
## Description
Use case is to import pretrained ONNX model and run inference in MXNet and similarly export MXNet model to [ONNX protobuf format](https://github.com/onnx/onnx).

So the idea is:
MXNet will have new module “serde”(temporary module name for serialization/deserialization purpose) which will support only ONNX format for now. (can add other formats later if needed)
And user should be just able to do the following to get MXNet model and parameters:

```
from mxnet.serde import serde
# import from onnx format
sym, params = serde.import_from(protofilepath, 'onnx')

# export to onnx format
protofile = serde.export_to(sym, params, 'onnx')
```
Any concerns or suggestions on this approach?

cc @lupesko @nswamy

issue_title: Link only necessary opencv libraries
issue_body:
Right now the build process of mxnet is linking all installed OpenCV libraries on the system. Or to be exact all libraries delivered by "pkg-config --cflags opencv". Wouldn't it be better to only add the necessary ones, otherwise mxnet will stop working if one of the opencv modules gets deleted later on.

```
# setup opencv
ifeq ($(USE_OPENCV), 1)
    CFLAGS += -DMXNET_USE_OPENCV=1 `pkg-config --cflags opencv`
    LDFLAGS += `pkg-config --libs opencv`
    BIN += bin/im2rec
else
    CFLAGS+= -DMXNET_USE_OPENCV=0
endif
```

issue_title: test_operator.test_rnnrelu_bidirectional has fixed seed that can mask flakiness

issue_title: Is there something wrong when full-connected layer calculate its gradient of weight?
issue_body:
In full_connected-int.h, Backward function, we see:

```
Tensor<xpu, 2, DType> gwmat = in_grad[fullc::kWeight].get<xpu, 2, DType>(s);
Assign(gwmat, req[fullc::kWeight], dot(grad.T(), data));
```

When we train models with batch size(m),  gwmat is the gradient of sum of m's gradient. 
Why not gwmat = dot(grad.T(), data)/m ?

issue_title: Broken Compatability with Python 2.7?
issue_body:
I noticed some recent commits that seem to break compatibility with Python 2.7. For example line 28 in `zmq/auth/asyncio.py`:

`events = yield from self.__poller.poll()`

It looks like "yield from" was only introduced in Python 3.3 as per https://docs.python.org/3/whatsnew/3.3.html .  One of the stated goals of PyZMQ is:

> PyZMQ should work with any Python ≥ 2.6 (including Python 3), as well as PyPy.

So I figured I'd let you know about these seemingly breaking changes.

issue_title: Dropout is too low
issue_body:
From the last run at epoch 19 we had a training WER of 13.0%.

However, at epoch 19 we also had a validation WER of 56.6%.

The gulf between the two is far too large. Thus, the dropout rate should be increased.

The original deep speech paper used a dropout range from 5-10%. We are currently at 5%.

**So, we should try 10% to get a feel for how the upper limit behaves.**

**We should also check the validation and training WER every 10 epochs to monitor.**

As you suggested in the docs, we use --local-scheduler only for testing purposes. Each member of our group works on his/her own computer (we have remote targets, so this setup works just great). Our central scheduler records the task history, as configured by our common luigi config file.

issue_title: Specifying a config file with an environment variable is broken

When setting the LUIGI_CONFIGURATION_PATH environment variable, my expectation is that the specified configuration will be the one used. However, it's simply added to the list of potential configurations instead. So if I have other configurations in the list, their values will be merged with the config I actually wanted.

This might make sense in some cases, but I think there should be a way to specify that you want a particular config without mixing in values from the global one.

issue_title: Parallel scheduling can't handle externalized dependencies
issue_body:
If you do something like
```
def requires(self):
    return luigi.task.externalize(SomeTask())
```
in a Task and then schedule it with the `--parallel-scheduling`, the scheduling will hang forever. The cause is that externalize sets `task.run = NotImplemented` and `NotImplemented` is not serializable so it can't be put in the multiprocessing queue.

My instinct is to fix this by just setting `task.run = None` or some other picklable value and treating that like NotImplemented in worker, but I'm afraid this will have weird side effects and leave the bug open for people implementing their own externalize functionality. Any other ideas?

Also, there's no indication that anything bad happened when this bug is triggered. We should find a way to catch this type of error and kill scheduling when it happens.

issue_title: Memory leak when filtering primitive types
Try again to remove tiling from the classical calculator
issue_body:
Tiling is terribly complicated (think only of what happens with the concept of effective ruptures) and causes subtle issues in corner cases, like it happened recently with Valerio's computation. The toxic combination is `tiling + optimize_same_id_sources=true`. Rather than fixing the bug, it is best to remove entirely the source of the computation. Tiling was essential in the past for two reasons:

1. performance in the prefiltering
2. avoiding slow tasks

Both issues have been mitigate in engine 3.0/3.1, so much that is now probably possible to remove the tiling functionality entirely. Some numbers to support the idea:

South America computation with 480,000 sites with tiles of 30,000 sites each:

```
   $ oq2 show performance 18067
   ============================== ======== ========= =======
   operation                      time_sec memory_mb counts 
   ============================== ======== ========= =======
   total classical                196,748  122       969    
   make_contexts                  117,157  0.0       217,179
   get_poes                       77,883   0.0       216,793
   total runtime                  1,444    520       1      
   managing sources               471      357       1      
   ============================== ======== ========= =======
   $ oq2 show task_info 18067
   ================== ==== ====== ======= === =========
   operation-duration mean stddev min     max num_tasks
   classical          203  85     1.58099 305 969      
   ================== ==== ====== ======= === =========
```

South America computation with 480,000 sites without tiling:
```
   michele@wilson:~$ oq2 show performance 18065
   ============================== ======== ========= ======
   operation                      time_sec memory_mb counts
   ============================== ======== ========= ======
   total classical                173,156  83        652   
   make_contexts                  96,632   0.0       93,656
   get_poes                       74,868   0.0       93,656
   total runtime                  1,542    1,110     1     
   managing sources               400      862       1     
   ============================== ======== ========= ======
   michele@wilson:~$ oq2 show task_info 18065
   ================== ==== ====== ======= === =========
   operation-duration mean stddev min     max num_tasks
   classical          265  106    2.31690 397 652      
   ================== ==== ====== ======= === =========
```
Nowadays tiling does not give us much. Actually the calculation time in the workers is worse with tiling.
It is best to remove it. In any case if was never fully functional (for instance, it was not implemented in event based).

issue_title: Multiple Content-Type headers on responses
issue_body:
All responses from the Api seem to have multiple content-type headers. For example 
when running the minimal API example and requesting with curl:

```
< HTTP/1.0 200 OK
< Content-Type: application/json
< Content-Length: 155
< Content-Type: application/vnd.api+json
< Server: Werkzeug/0.14.1 Python/2.7.14
```

I believe this is caused by 8ca7a74b9e0a4c66c4408c31c1f8f285e6cadd74 as `jsonify` returns a new `Response` object with `mimetype` set to `current_app.config['JSONIFY_MIMETYPE']` (defaults to `'application/json'`). This object is then passed to `make_response` along with the correct content-type which ends up only extending the existing headers. Maybe use `flask.json.dumps` instead of `flask.json.jsonify`?

issue_title: Docs for IAM Role collections: policies vs attached_policies
issue_body:
After durdling around a bit I _think_ I understand this, and may recommend an improvement to the documentation... 

**Doc Change 1:**
The [documentation for the IAM Role collection attached_policies](https://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/services/iam.html#IAM.Role.attached_policies) should read:

> A list of managed policies attached to the role.

wording from [AWS IAM REST API doc for RoleDetails, AttachedManagedPolicies.member.N](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/APIReference/API_RoleDetail.html).

Instead of:

> A collection of Policy resources

**Doc Change 2:**
The [documentation for the IAM Role collection policies](https://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/services/iam.html#IAM.Role.policies) should read:

> A list of inline policies embedded in the role.

wording from [AWS IAM REST API doc for RoleDetails, RolePolicyList.member.N](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/APIReference/API_RoleDetail.html).

Instead of:

> A collection of RolePolicy resources

**Motivation:**
These changes may help someone (like me) who is looking for ways to (for instance) delete an IAM Role, which, it turns out:

* requires you to delete/detach the Role Policies first, which, it turns out:
* is done differently for _inline_ policies (see [IAM Client delete_role_policy](https://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/services/iam.html#IAM.Client.delete_role_policy) ) and _managed_ policies (see [IAM Client detach_role_policy](https://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/services/iam.html#IAM.Client.detach_role_policy) ), which, it turns out:
* requires you to get a list of the policies first, which you might get via [ListRolePolicies Paginator](https://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/services/iam.html#IAM.Paginator.ListRolePolicies) (yay!), which, it turns out:
* A) returns a list of Policy Names (whereas the delete_role_policy and detach_role_policy APis require the Policy ARNs) (_sad_panda_), and
* B) does not expose a way to distinguish between inline and managed policies, which, it turns out:
* B1) is not exposed by [the RolePolicy resource](https://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/services/iam.html#rolepolicy) (_sadder_panda_), and
* B2) is not exposed by [IAM Client get_role_policy](https://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/services/iam.html#IAM.Client.get_role_policy) either (_raging_panda_)

issue_title: process.extract*: Inconsistent behaviour between python2 and python3 when using pandas.Series
issue_body:
When executing the following code, `process.extract*` functions do not behave consistently.

```
from fuzzywuzzy import process
import pandas as pd

a = pd.Series.from_array(['abc', 'def', 'ghi'])
process.extractOne('abd', a)
```

In python2 (python 2.7.12, pandas 0.20.3, fuzzywuzzy 0.15.1) the output is:
```
('abc', 67,)
```
In python3 (python 3.5.2, pandas 0.20.3, fuzzywuzzy 0.15.1) the output is:
```
('abc', 67, 0)
```

So apparently (following the documentation in process.p), fuzzywuzzy under python3 interprets `pd.Series` as a dictionary and not as a list-like object. 

Not entirely sure if the problem is on the fuzzy side - but some users might get confused when migrating from python2 to python3.

issue_title: Press Key is not working as argument for run_keyword function if send 'Enter' (\13)
issue_body:
test.robot
```
*** Settings ***
Documentation     This test will ...
Library           Selenium2Library
Library           keywords.py
Test Teardown     Close All Browsers

*** Test Cases ***
    Create Webdriver  Safari  executable_path=${technology_preview}
    Maximize Browser Window
    Set Selenium Speed  .5 seconds
    Go to  http://google.com
    Search it
```

keywords.py
```
from robot.libraries.BuiltIn import BuiltIn
import pdb

bi = BuiltIn()
s2l = bi.get_library_instance('Selenium2Library')

def search_it():
    bi.run_keyword('Input Text', 'id=lst-ib', 'biology')
    bi.run_keyword('Press Key', 'id=lst-ib', '\13') # does nothing! Expected: It should press "Enter" key
    pdb.set_trace()
```

Update:
as per @pekkaklarck comment:

> Yeah, on a closer look that problem is that \ is an escape character in Python and \13 is actually same as \x0b i.e. byte 13. As commented above, you need to escape \ and use \\13 instead.

I have tried both `\` and `\\` before posting it here. Same actual result. Already double checked it on 3 different sites for 3 different browsers. It is not working if it sent by `run_keyword()` function. But if it's in *.robot file like `Press Key | \\13` then it is working fine
```
bi.run_keyword('Input Text', 'xpath=//*[@id="q"]', anything)
bi.run_keyword('Press Key', 'xpath=//*[@id="q"]', '\\13')
```
Actual output/result:
```
[ INFO    ] Wait until element 'xpath=//*[@id="q"]' is visible.
[ INFO    ] Typing text 'lipstick' into text field 'xpath=//*[@id="q"]'
[ INFO    ] Wait until element 'xpath=//*[@id="q"]' is visible.
```
Pass. No enter key was pressed

issue_title: item_sort_key always uses itemgetter in 3.11
issue_body:
Got a strange but when upgrading 3.10.0 to 3.11.0 via pip.

A test started failing that used a custom sort function via `item_sort_key`. It seems that setting this kwarg has the effect of enabling sorting using the default `itemgetter` in 3.11.0.

This occurs when using the C encoder implementation.

Python is 2.7.11 on Ubuntu 16.04.

Using this code:

    import simplejson

    def sortit(x):
      return x[1]

    a = {
      "c": "3",
      "b": "1",
      "a": "2"
    }

    print simplejson.dumps(a, item_sort_key=sortit)

On 3.10.0:

    $ python test.js
    {"b": "1", "c": "2", "a": "3"}

And on 3.11.0:

    $ python test.js
    {"a": "2", "b": "1", "c": "3"}

issue_title: OSError when creating virtualenv symlinking in Vagrant
issue_body:
### The issue
Using pipenv in a vagrant box is causing a little trouble with the symlinking.
It has been request that `--always-copy` is added to pipenv when it creates the virtual environment see ( #1929) but it appears that is not gonna happen 😞 

### The error
```
Using /usr/bin/python3.6m (3.6.5) to create virtualenv…
Running virtualenv with interpreter /usr/bin/python3.6m
Using base prefix '/usr'
New python executable in /vagrant/project/thing/.venv/bin/python3.6m
Also creating executable in /vagrant/project/thing/.venv/bin/python
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/dist-packages/virtualenv.py", line 2349, in <module>
    main()
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/dist-packages/virtualenv.py", line 712, in main
    symlink=options.symlink)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/dist-packages/virtualenv.py", line 927, in create_environment
    site_packages=site_packages, clear=clear, symlink=symlink))
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/dist-packages/virtualenv.py", line 1395, in install_python
    os.symlink(py_executable_base, full_pth)
OSError: [Errno 71] Protocol error: 'python3.6m' -> '/vagrant/project/thing/.venv/bin/python'
```

### Environment variables
```bash
export PIPENV_VENV_IN_PROJECT=1
export PIPENV_IGNORE_VIRTUALENVS=1
```
### commands to fix it
```bash
pipenv sync # Fails with OSError listed above
virtualenv --always-copy .venv
pipenv sync # Success!
```

Seen in vagrant box: `ubuntu/xenial64` and `ubuntu/bionic64`

I hope it helps someone who got stuck with this like I did

issue_title: get_default_cache writes to a roaming profile directory in recent Windows by default
issue_body:
In get_default_cache the first choice of directory to write to on Windows is given by %APPDATA%.  In Windows Vista this is part of the roaming profile for the user, not the local one, i.e. it writes to C:\Users\username\AppData\Roaming, not C:\Users\username\AppData\Local - see https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/dd378457(v=vs.85).aspx.

This increases the roaming profile size and causes Windows login delays for users.

It may be best to add %LOCALAPPDATA% as the first possible directory to write to.

issue_title: easy_install goes down hard with nasty traceback when trying to install latest setuptools version
issue_body:
This is a problem we're seeing on SLES11 SP3, where setuptools is provided via the `python-setuptools-0.6c11-6.1` package.

While installing a tool that has a dependency on `setuptools>0.6` (the 0.6c11 version is considered older, so an update is attempted), the following happens.

Note how this quickly goes into lala-land and (luckily) ends up in a `maximum recursion depth` error rather than causing more trouble.

This is a very new problem, until recently this worked just fine, so a change in a recent release of `setuptools` seems to be causing this (even though the bug that makes this happen is in the `easy_install` or `setuptools` installation available on the system, that's not clear to me).

Questions:

* What's going on here exactly? Is this fixable (in a `setuptools` update)?

* Any idea which recent change introduced this problem? Can we come up with a stricter version requirement (i.e., a upper limit on the setuptools version) to (for now) dance around this problem?

* I suspect part of the answer is going to be "don't use `easy_install` anymore, use `pip` instead"... Is my hunch correct? If so, what is the recommendation for systems where `easy_install` has worked fine until recently and where `pip` is not readily available?

cc @dominikborkowski

issue_title: package_dir in setup.cfg
issue_body:
Setuptools version: 38.4.0
Python version: 2.7.12
Ubuntu version: 16.04

Hey! I'm moving all my options from setup.py to setup.cfg so I can have a clean setup.py:

```
"""Minimal setup.py."""
from setuptools import setup
from setuptools.config import read_configuration

conf_dict = read_configuration('setup.cfg')

setup()
```

But I'm having a problem with the package_dir option.

My package lives inside src folder and while I can do `package_dir={"":"src"}` in the setup.py, but I can't use it properly in the setup.cfg.

What I tried so far? (I tried more combinations, but they give an error)
```
[options]
package_dir =
    . = src
```
```
[options]
package_dir =
    "" = "src"
```
```
[options]
package_dir =
   "."  = src
```

With this options, when I run `python setup.py egg_info` it leads to.. "package directory <package_name> not found".

Any insight? Thank you :D

issue_title: twistd klein
issue_body:
Thus spoke @glyph, in March of 2012:

"I want to take the example at the top of the readme, clip off the 'run' function at the bottom, then do

    twistd klein myklein.py --port tcp:8080

so as to get all the usual goodness of listening on an endpoint.

Although what I *really* want is the ability to do

    twistd klein --personal

just like I can do

    twistd web --personal ...

So I can run awesome klein apps on twistedmatrix.com."

issue_title: Selector.root is not an instance of lxml.html.HtmlElement even if parser is html
issue_body:
I'm trying to use lxml.Cleaner without parsing response multiple times:

```py
from lxml.html.clean import Cleaner
cleaner = Cleaner()
sel = parsel.Selector("<html><body><style>.p {width:10px}</style>hello</body></html>"
cleaner.clean_html(sel.root)
```

This doesn't work because Cleaner needs a `lxml.html.HtmlElement` instance, while Selector.root is always `lxml.etree._Element`, so it doesn't have a required `.rewrite_links` method.

Why is lxml.etree.HtmlParser [used](https://github.com/scrapy/parsel/blob/fde9087b3bbc8adf54ad25b9ea68874054adaec2/parsel/selector.py#L20) for html and not lxml.html.HtmlParser?

issue_title: AsyncIOBackend logs 'unclosed client session' error on each trigger()
issue_body:
`pusher==1.7.3` and `aiohttp==2.3.9`

`pusher.trigger` any message, and you'll see the following error if you have logging enabled (this is reported as an error level event to any attached tracing software etc, so it generates a lot of spam):

```
Unclosed client session
client_session: <aiohttp.client.ClientSession object at 0x7f3c9004dba8>
```

issue_title: Malformed query string when filter on multiple statuses
issue_body:
Trying to filter on multiple statuses results in a malformed query string in Python3.  The loop over the query_params gets evaluated at each iteration resulting in the `[]` being added multiple times.

```
DEBUG:requests.packages.urllib3.connectionpool:https://api.pagerduty.com:443 "GET /incidents?sort_by=created_at%3Adesc&offset=0&limit=1&statuses%5B%5D%5B%5D%5B%5D%5B%5D%5B%5D%5B%5D%5B%5D%5B%5D=triggered&statuses%5B%5D%5B%5D%5B%5D%5B%5D%5B%5D%5B%5D%5B%5D%5B%5D=acknowledged
```

issue_title: cant define custom resource definitions
issue_body:
when trying to define the following 

```
apiVersion: apiextensions.k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: CustomResourceDefinition
metadata:
  labels:
    kubevirt.io: ''
  name: virtualmachines.kubevirt.io
spec:
  group: kubevirt.io
  names:
    kind: VirtualMachine
    plural: virtualmachines
    shortNames:
    - vm
    - vms
    singular: virtualmachine
  scope: Namespaced
  version: v1alpha1
```

using this ansible module

issue_title: Cast non-string objects to string types
issue_body:
I previously hit this problem in this library, [fixed it](https://github.com/segmentio/analytics-python/commit/6310705df96fb8ee2faf0538443528f4b08a6850) and my PR was merged. Since then it seems like you rewrote much of it and reintroduced the problem. The problem is basically [with this line](https://github.com/segmentio/analytics-python/blob/fca319715715f54390960f017524e6c8117492d7/analytics/client.py#L204) of code. Here's the scenario:

```
>>> from decimal import Decimal
>>> from six import string_types
>>> isinstance("100.00", string_types)
True
>>> isinstance(unicode(Decimal("100.00")), string_types)
True
>>>
>>> isinstance(Decimal("100.00"), string_types)
False
```

There is no reason to reject decimal types. They serialize to JSON-compatible strings perfectly well. In short: many, many objects developers deal with in python are not strings but can still be serialized out and you probably shouldn't be so aggressive about rejecting them. This happens with Decimals, custom types, lazy localization strings, etc... Your users can all pepper their code with `unicode()` calls to explicitly cast them into the types you need for your JSON transport but the proper way to solve this would be for you to just try casting to whatever types your API expects/support for serialization.

I am not 100% sure but I think this is also the problem outlined in issue #39.

issue_title: RFC 'Restrict further searches to this collection' checkbox
issue_body:
Originally on 2011-01-19

(Quoted verbatim from a mail I wrote to the -users list 9 jan 2009)

Hi all,

I have been thinking on a search option, easy to implement, that could benefit a lot current Invenio sites.

As you know, you can start searching an Invenio site at any collection, and the first search is effectively restricted to this collection.  But as soon as you start clicking hyperlinks, the searches are no longer restricted to this original collection.  The current software allows, of course, to restrict afterwards those searches to any collection via the pulldown menu of the list of all collections, but in many installations this list is quite long, and not practical to do more than once or twice.  Adding a '&c=mycollection' to the url is sometimes faster, but we cannot expect to do that to everybody.  And indeed, there are cases that creating such an isolated view of the site is useful and desirable.

The solution I've come up with is to add a checkbox next to the search field with the text *Restrict further searches to this collection* or similar.  If checked the collection name would be 'sticky' until unchecked again.

I think that it would be relatively easy to implement.  Unfortunately, I cannot do much of it now, except of asking your opinion about it.

What do you think?

Thanks,

Ferran

issue_title: Add fields/interfaces for the numeric tower
issue_body:
Similar to #11 and its implementation in #47 where we added fields and interfaces for `collections.Sequence`, `collections.MutableSequence`, `collections.Mapping` and`collections.MutableMapping`, I think it makes sense to have fields and interfaces for `numbers.Number`, `numbers.Real`, `numbers.Rational` and `numbers.Integral`.

In our own usage, we commonly parse data from JSON, and depending on the browser/client, we can get floats or ints for the same field, so we use a `Number` field that allows `numbers.Number`. But it's badly specified, what we *really* want is a `numbers.Real` field.

issue_title: Uses BTrees wrongly, breaks when Interfaces stop being available
issue_body:
Unfortunately, I could not find all kind of problems related to this, but this far I got:
The RelationCatalog stores InterfaceClasses in Btrees. These Interface class implementations can get lost if a package gets really uninstalled. ZODB in such a case manages, and creates a dynamic class on the fly. It has ZODB.Brocken or something in its __mro__. These classes have a different sort order and won't be found in BTrees any longer.
They appear in the BTree if you iterate over it, but you won't get it via getitem.
You could repair it by creating copies, but then you cannot store the RelationCatalog in the ZODB, because it won't store broken objects.
This package should either be able to handle the case of missing classes, or have repair methods or stops using classes that can change between restarts.

issue_title: creating new service does not start the service
issue_body:
Dear all,

I would like to create a new service that starts when PythonService starts.
What I did was to create an aidl, java class to implement the Service interface and extend the Stub in the same package as PythonService. 

I modified the manifest xml template to expose the service in the same process as PythonService, but still the service is not started.

When using the same code in another pure Java application the service starts well, a client can bind to it.

Can you tell me what key steps am I missing.

Thanks in advance for your help.

Ancuta Corici

issue_title: One file per datareader
issue_body:
Hello,

`data.py` was a very (too) big file.

I think we should have one file per datareader.

Moreover this will help https://github.com/pydata/pandas-datareader/issues/48

I've done on my fork a branch where each datareader is a file.

    $ nosetests -s -v

shows

    Ran 58 tests in 83.806s
    FAILED (SKIP=7, errors=10, failures=3)

that's exactly same result with this `master` branch.

I will send a PR.

Kind regards

issue_title: os.path.dirname(__file__) returns empty
issue_body:
In the examples there are 2 examples for writing to the so-called database (in this case, generate a text file). This did not work on windows (perhaps this has nothing to do with this being on windows, but more todo with looking into the current directory), I had to change the code from
    `os.path.dirname(__file__)`
to
    `os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__))`
to get it to work.

Link to stackoverflow question that helped me find this : http://stackoverflow.com/questions/7783308/os-path-dirname-file-returns-empty

issue_title: Outdated PyPi version
issue_body:
It appears installing the fire module using pip produces an [outdated version](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/fire/0.1.0) of the library. I've run into an error identical to #6, and after checking the result of that, it appears the update that fixed #6 can only be installed by cloning the repository and running setup.py, as suggested.

FYI, I was using fire version 0.1.0 with Python 3.5.2 when installed using pip.

issue_title: dump datasets performance: use package_search
issue_body:
with package_search we can dump all datasets in far fewer API calls.

issues:
- ckan < 2.2 returns different dataset data from package_search and package_show so we'll need to maintain the old code as well
- we need to request the datasets ordered by id, not modification date, so that we know we have a complete dump and to replicate the current behaviour
- ckan sites may have limited the number of packages returned from package_search in different ways, maybe detect the limit and work with what we're given, or just revert to package_show method?

issue_title: User don't have KEY field which breaks integrity
issue_body:
jira.group_members("jira-users") returns list of users where KEY field is actually NOT a user's key but a user's name!
But this field - user's name can be changed in JIRA by administrator and this causes big problem if we want to extract the data and keep the references between the entities.

Example:
1.you exported users to your database
2.you exported issues and linked them to the users (you should use issue.fields.assignee.name, as you cannot use fields.assignee.key because you don't know the user's keys)
3.you changed user's name
4.you exported new users (new user is created which is actually a duplicate)
5. you trying to sync issues and link them to users and new issues are linked to new user.
Now you have duplicate users both with issues!

Suggestion:
jira.group_members() should use user's key for keys and user's name field should be among other user's fields together with email and fullname.

JIRA's API already supports this - 
"/rest/api/2/group?groupname=#{groupname}&expand=users"  returns JSON with users' keys.

Please fix this.

issue_title: Pip freeze to requirements.txt creates chalice deploy error on Python 3.6.1
issue_body:
Steps to reproduce. Python 3.6.1 installed via homebrew on Mac OS 10.12.6, although the error seems to be reproducible with Python 2.7 as well:

1. Create a new python3 virtual environment and install chalice
$python3 -m venv ./env
$source ./env/bin/activate
$pip install chalice
(which installs typing as a dependency)

2. Create a new chalice project and deploy
$chalice new-project freeze-test
$cd freeze-test
$chalice deploy
(deploys correctly as expected)

3. Freeze current requirements and redeploy
$pip freeze > requirements.txt
requirements.txt is now:
 
```
botocore==1.6.5
chalice==1.0.1
click==6.6
docutils==0.14
jmespath==0.9.3
python-dateutil==2.6.1
six==1.10.0
typing==3.5.3.0

$chalice deploy

You will have to build these yourself and vendor them in
the chalice vendor folder.

Your deployment will continue but may not work correctly
if missing dependencies are not present. For more information:
http://chalice.readthedocs.io/en/latest/topics/packaging.html

…then it hangs updating the lambda function.
With chalice —debug deploy, it hangs at:

`config-ssh` is extremely useful for easily accessing hosts with public IPs.

If a project has an SSH bastion host, adding a `ProxyCommand` directive to hosts with only private IPs would allow automatically connecting to them through a user specified bastion host.

issue_title: Pub/Sub subscriptions don't bind to the specified topic as stated in documentation
issue_body:
Page Name: pubsub-topic
Release: 0.18.1

Can a helper `subscription` method be added in the `gcloud.pubsub.client.Client` class to facilitate subscribing to subscriptions similar to how there's a `topic` method to facilitate creating topics. Current [instructions](https://cloud.google.com/pubsub/docs/subscriber#receiving-pull-messages) for receiving pull messages suggest invoking the `gcloud.pubsub.topic.Topic.subscription` method which creates a subscription bound to the specified topic as follows:

```
pubsub_client = pubsub.Client()
topic = pubsub_client.topic(topic_name)
subscription = topic.subscription(subscription_name)

results = subscription.pull(return_immediately=True)
```

However a non-existent topic name can be supplied for `topic_name` and messages can still be received from the `results`.

The following code also works without having to specify a topic:

```
pubsub_client = pubsub.Client()
subscription = pubsub.subscription.Subscription(subscription_name, client=pubsub_client)
```

The `gcloud.pubsub.topic.Topic.subscription` documentation stating _"Creates a subscription bound to the current topic"_ is misleading because it seems it binds to all topics.

issue_title: Batch upload fails when messages exceed API limit
issue_body:
1. OS type and version
All OS and all versions supporting topic.Batch
2. Steps to reproduce
Create a batch context & publish messages to it
On batch commit an API failure occurs due to batch size being too large

Suggestion:
In the publish function check the serialised size of the message does not exceed 10MB and that the number of messages doesn't exceed 1000. If it does then commit the batch prematurely and clear the batch queue.

I've crudely implemented it in my own project (just for size, not quantity):
```
class SizedBatch(topic.Batch):
    def __init__(self, topic, client):
        super(SizedBatch, self).__init__(topic, client)
        self.messages_size = 0

    def publish(self, message, **attrs):
        self.topic._timestamp_message(attrs)
        item = {'data': message, 'attributes': attrs}
        self.messages.append(item)
        self.messages_size += len(base64.b64encode(json.dumps(item, ensure_ascii=False).encode('utf-8')))
        if self.messages_size > 95e5:
            self.commit()
            self.messages_size = 0
```
Of course this adds the published message to the queue before checking, allowing a case in which the message creates the API violation but from my own observations the API accepts messages of 15MB and sometimes of 20MB. This also does not take into account the other aspects of the message (which are largely insignificant compared to the message content).

issue_title: Improve performance of SelectDependencies(.., transitive=True)
issue_body:
About 30% of total runtime is spent in `store_list` while merging `HydratedTargets` up to the root. This codepath uses `SelectDependencies(.., transitive=True)` [here](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/src/python/pants/engine/legacy/graph.py#L325-L329).

The implementation of `SelectDependencies(.., transitive=True)` is to recursively select the dependencies of some type, and use `store_list(.., merge=True)` to merge them. The problem with this is that (currently) rather than merging all of the transitive deps into a single mutable set, we create and then throw away a ton of temporary sets.

A recommended approach would be to move more of the merge logic to the rust side. In particular `SelectDependencies(.., transitive=True)` might deserve its own return type, which might allow the whole thing to be implemented using [future::loop](http://alexcrichton.com/futures-rs/futures/future/fn.loop_fn.html) like the IO patch does [here](https://github.com/twitter/pants/blob/7b59a4df1a109f99bcfcbb7b025f0a338eeb3b30/src/rust/engine/src/fs.rs#L544). That approach would probably involve using `OrderMap`, again similar to what the IO loop does.

issue_title: Make ivy and coursier cache links snapshottable
issue_body:
This code for ivy:
https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/5f36f1548023b28a8c6ecbf330b2612aefa22fef/src/python/pants/backend/jvm/ivy_utils.py#L829-L871

And this code for coursier: https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/5f36f1548023b28a8c6ecbf330b2612aefa22fef/src/python/pants/backend/jvm/tasks/coursier_resolve.py#L612-L614

Make trees of symlinks pointing at jar files in central cache directories.

Unfortunately, symlinks don't play nicely with v2 snapshotting and remote execution.

Long-term, we're going to want to put these fetches natively into v2, but short term, we need to be able to get at these files using the existing codepaths.

Let's change the behavior here so that we don't have symlinks. The desired end-state is:
If we can hardlink where we currently symlink, do so.
If not, copy the file.

Probably the easiest way to do this is to attempt to hardlink, and if that throws, instead copy.

Let's not worry about replacing existing symlinks; I think it's reasonable for people to have to `clean-all` one time if they're starting to use remote execution.

issue_title: Make monolithic jar slimmer
issue_body:
Monolithic jar does not scale, even with --no-deployjar that excludes external
jars. In Twitter we saw a few hundred Mbytes jars to transfer, for remote
offices this is painful.

The API change includes:

* keep --deploy-jar as is, make --no-deployjar no longer bundle internal
libraries, instead, make them symlinks under libs, link to the library jars
under workdir, but with stable names, and embed these stable symlinks in the
Manifest's Class-Path attribute.

* One side effect of the above change is shading can now only be applied to
--deploy-jar mode, since we don't want to change anything that's under workdir.
If anyone thinks this is unacceptable, we can do copy on change, please let
me know.

issue_title: Add support for resources to GoWorkspaceTask
issue_body: Currently Go has no official way to deal with resource files.  Since pants constructs parallel workspaces under .pants.d, this means any relative path expectations used by non-pantsed Go code will fail.  To work around this / work with this, pants should respect `resources(...)` dependencies in Go targets when building a workspace symlink farm or else have a looser interpretation of acceptable files owned by a go target and just glob ~everything when building a workspace.

issue_title: hypotheses lengths off by one
issue_body:
It seems that the current beam search implementation (`_beam_search`) does not compute hypotheses length correctly. This can be seen when printing `sequences` and `lengths` just before returning from `_beam_search()`: the length is always one short of the actual non-zero values in sequences:

```
> sequences[0]
[    2    92  7741   907  2710   295    28     7  7741   907  2710     4
     7   383 10174  7324  9735   286   671   564  4085    71    22    98
 10671     4    13   782   485     8  6323  8665     4     8    23     8
  2976  1037  2448  3577    10   294   575  4664    13    34  3898   608
    43     5     3     0]
<NDArray 52 @cpu(0)>

> lengths[0]
[50.]
<NDArray 1 @cpu(0)>
```
`3` is the EOS id and the length of this hypothesis is clearly 51.

Let me try to explain the problem below. In practice, it doesn't matter since `_assemble_translation()` uses the length value to cut off the EOS symbol; the following EOS stripping logic in `_make_result()` is then redundant. But of course it shouldn't be and I think `_beam_search()` should return consistent data structures.

Here's the issue; I think this was introduced with length normalization only done for finished hypotheses:
1. `lengths` is initialized with 1s to account for the initial data in sequences, namely the BOS symbol
2. In each beam search iteration: as soon as we run topk on hypotheses scores, we effectively have expanded an hypothesis and its length increases by 1.
3. However, we are not using the updated length to potentially normalize the hypothesis if its finished. We check the newly acquired best_word_indices for EOS/PAD to compute a current `finished` status, and if so, **we normalize with the OLD length**.
4. This doesn't yet explain why we end beam_search with a length off by 1. This must somehow happen because the `length` array is only updated for hypotheses that are not finished and active. Since `finished` was just recomputed in step 3, the problem must lie in the `inactive` logic or the fact that right after we update `lengths`, we recompute `finished` once again and determine breaking out of the beam search loop based on that.

I am not 100% sure whats wrong here, probably the order of operations isn't entirely correct, but I also don't fully understand the whole `inactive` logic introduced. @mjpost could you comment on this?

At a minimum, we should make sure to normalize with correct length values. At best, we should make sure to return a `lengths` array from `_beam_search` that is consistent with `sequences`. With that, `_assemble_translation` would not silently cut off the last token (EOS), which by design should be handled later by `_make_result()`.

issue_title: add RTL helper for inline SVGs

Many of our inline SVGs need to be flipped based on the `isRtl` status.
